item.title: And now we wait........
item.description: I'm a fan of pineapple on pizza (or rather, letting people enjoy things that they like), and I just wanted to stir the pot. Front page edit: Wow. There are tens of us out there!! TENS!! Who among you ordered a pizza because of this post? All of your suggestions sounded amazing and ended up ordering one myself. Bacon, Pineapple, Jalapenos, and red Onions. It's glorious. Anyways, be good to each other. We all might like different pizzas, but we can all still be at the same party.
item.dc:creator: ohgodthehorror

item.title: Greed.
item.dc:creator: doxydad1

item.title: Freddie Oversteegen, what an absolute legend!
item.description: She died in September 2018, however I never heard of her until now and I really think her story should be shared everywhere, absolute legend. Fuck the Nazis! (originally posted by @DumpingSh1tandBeingKind2 on 2023-08-20 18:15:06)
item.dc:creator: ThisTimeLastYear

item.title: Some Tech Advice
item.description:
My personal favorite. Example being https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OlCqYyAQE_w turns into http://www.ssyoutube.com/watch?v=OlCqYyAQE_w
I personally dislike this one. I prefer the one in the first picture. An example being https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OlCqYyAQE_w turns into http://deturl.com/youtube.com/watch?v=OlCqYyAQE_w
link: http://ocw.mit.edu/index.htm
If you don't believe me all you have to do is make a copy of your presentation and change that one. It's much faster in the classroom setting and makes you look tech savvy.
I can't verify this one and quite frankly I don't want a random extension. There used to be a way to right click the video stream and allow youtube to download the video continuously instead of stopping at a certain point. As I was making this list I discovered it is no longer present. Correct me if I'm wrong.
https://translate.google.com/ As far as I know you can't just use it to translate an english webpage into english. You'll have to translate the english website into another language. Should be easy if your viewing something like imgur. Lucky for us Google is nice with translating http://prntscr.com/4erfy8
link: http://ocw.mit.edu/index.htm (originally posted by @player1673 in r/lifehacks on 2014-08-20 19:44:33)
item.dc:creator: ThisTimeLastDecade

item.title: Top 10 Best Sci fi indie movies from last years
item.description:
The Artifice Girl (2022) - A team of special agents discovers a revolutionary new computer program to bait and trap online predators. After teaming up with the program’s troubled developer, they soon find that the AI is rapidly advancing beyond its original purpose.
If You Were the Last (2023) - Adrift in their broken-down space shuttle with little hope of rescue, two astronauts argue over whether they’re better off spending their remaining days as friends or something more.
Mondays: See You ‘This’ Week! (2022) - Akemi Yoshikawa discovers that her whole office is stuck in a time loop, repeating the same stressful work week over and over. Together with her colleagues she has to find out how to break the loop.
Jules (2023) - A flying saucer lands in the backyard of an elderly suburbanite with memory problems, who forms a bond with the scared alien inside.
Synchronic (2019) - Two New Orleans paramedics’ lives are ripped apart after encountering a series of horrific deaths linked to a designer drug with bizarre, otherworldly effects.
They Cloned Tyrone (2023) - A series of eerie events thrusts an unlikely trio onto the trail of a nefarious government conspiracy lurking directly beneath their neighborhood.
Significant Other (2022) - Ruth and Harry decide to take a romantic backpacking trip through the Pacific Northwest, but amongst the beautiful scenery, Ruth makes an unexpected discovery that sets her off on a strange, frightening new path. The couple aren’t alone in the woods, and they might not be the same when they come out…if they come out.
The Antares Paradox (2022) - In a race against the clock, Alexandra will be forced to choose how to spend the next hours: facing a critic family drama or trying to bring an answer to one of humanity’s most important questions.
No One Will Save You (2023) - An exiled anxiety-ridden homebody must battle an alien who’s found its way into her home.
Palm Springs (2020) - When carefree Nyles and reluctant maid of honor Sarah have a chance encounter at a Palm Springs wedding, things get complicated when they find themselves unable to escape the venue, themselves, or each other. (Someone posted an old top 10 scifi indie movies that I did 10 years ago, so I did a new one, I hope you like it )

item.title: My sweater collection is funded! (Thanks to you guys!!!!)
item.description: For a bit of transparency, I had a hard month. I’m dealing with my own health struggles, my husband fractured his spine, I got deeply sick and still had to take care of him because he couldn’t walk. It was tough lol and I didn’t make this project was going to make it much higher, because there was a bit of a time crunch but it totally did! Thanks so much for your awesome support. I’m genuinely, deeply grateful. For anyone interested, these are still available for just a little over 24 hours: https://www.backerkit.com/c/projects/echonyx/Timeless-Threads

Fun stuff. Teaching through lunch and working late this evening. Thriving. Send anything uplifting, same name on discord.
item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: Ten years ago, I started a project that launched my down the rabbit hole of capital punishment in Texas. It had an extremely powerful effect on me, and I’d like to share it with you.
item.description:
THIS POST IS SUPER LONG, MY APOLOGIES!! . I've tried to make this post a couple of times before, but I'd always end up taking it down because the comments section and even my private inbox became an exhausting battlefield. However, for the tenth anniversary of this project, I decided to try to post this one more time. Especially since I now have a better idea on how to present it. . In 2014, I began an extensive project that involved following the numbers on Texas death row. I wanted to know the breakdown of the types of **convictions** that resulted in a death sentence. For now, this is an unending project. Texas is the state with the most frequent executions. Currently, 589 people have been executed, and more are going to follow. I don’t think it’s possible to do an accurate data analysis of Texas death row anymore. Despite that, I still keep track of the changing data. That’s because over time this project became more intimate. When I began sifting through the written last statements of the people who were executed, I felt a weird sense of connection. To be clear: regardless of who it was, I didn't sympathize with the crime. There are also many inmates that I don't sympathize with at all. Others I sympathize with to a degree, especially a few cases that had me raising an eyebrow at the fairness of their trial. As I got deeper into the project, I started forgetting what I was looking for. In the end, I found something else. It was something that went beyond a data table. It was something so undescribably human. That experience changed me, and now I'd like to share that experience with you.
I cannot emphasize enough that the contents of this post are disturbing. People go on death row for capital murder with special circumstances. Every victim was murdered. Some include sexual assault. Others involve the murder of a child. Please read with caution. It doesn't make you weak if this post is too intense to handle. With that out of the way, there are two more things I want to address before getting into the post.
Link to executed offenders without my captions: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_executed_offenders.html ^ The other thing you can do if you’re on mobile is press on one image, and then swipe through them until you reach the end. . . These are the frequently asked questions that I've received on my other attempts to make this post. The biggest complaint is my refusal to take a side, and my lack of focus on innocent people on death row. . Whether capital punishment could/should be reformed, or if it should be abolished altogether is an important conversation to have. That being said, that’s not the focus of THIS post. That's a conversation for a different day. I have an opinion on capital punishment, but I don't want to discuss it in THIS post. Here's why: I am afraid of the effect it would have. I don't want these last statements to be drowned by people that are angered by my opinion. Experience has taught me that readers would become more focused on trying to prove me wrong, rather than experiencing these last statements. . That being said, I'm not a propagandist. There are a couple of last statements that are shrouded in extra controversy, and I explain why. I'm just not going to say "And they were right/wrong to execute them anyway!" because that's a conversation for another time.
Link: https://imgur.com/a/3JdxHBp . Something that I failed to include in the previous image is the layout of these last statements. If I was going to make this a non-issue post, I had to include both sides. If I could find a picture and description or obituary of the victim, I included it in a follow up image. The people that died in this post are dead because someone else died first. It's not just their perspective that matters. Now we can move onto the actual last statements.
I figured I'd start from the beginning. Charlie Brooks was the first man to be executed in the State of Texas after capital punishment was reinstated. You can sort of tell because they allowed him a good amount of time to speak. Later on, last words would become shorter. Charlie Brook’s last statement is purely religious, so I don’t relate to it much. But he was the first. Something that I’ve always wondered is why he was praising the glory of God who he claimed would determine if he was about to die from lethal injection? I don’t think a God that allows you to be executed doesn’t seem like a God that is on your side. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/brookscharlie.html
Headstone Inscription: "Beloved Husband and Father" . Image Source: https://fwtx.com/magazines/fort-worth-magazine/the-unforgivable-charlie-brooks/ Find A Grave: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/107346405/david-preston-gregory
I am flabbergasted by the sheer tastelessness of this statement. When I came across Thomas Barefoot's last statement, I just blinked at my laptop screen for a few moments, trying to comprehend his thought process here. I don't think he said this to be sadistic. There are far worse things he could've said if he was aiming for that. That being said, I think it was self-centered, and lacking any real remorse. There is no world in where telling a widow of the husband you killed “will go to hell for not forgiving me” is a gracious and kind thing to say. I can’t imagine being Mrs. Levin, and listening to her husband’s killer tell her that the grief that he caused her would send her to hell. As if this whole experience couldn’t have been more traumatic than it already was. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/barefootthomas.jpg
The bio I found was in all caps for some reason... Worked forTHE HARKER HEIGHTS P.D. HAS A CITY PARK NAMED FOR HIM IN HARKER HEIGHTS . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/11842846/carl-irving-levin
In every crime, big or small, there are two sides: the victim and their family, and the perpetrator and their family. Many times, the family sticks by the victim/perpetrator, but not always. Everyone has a tolerance meter. For the Skillern Family, shooting a cop that was trying to bust him for the drugs that he was choosing to use/distribute was simply unforgivable. Disappointing family is a unique type of pain, and it's clear that Doyle was feeling it. Imagine knowing (or at least believing) that your death will be a source of joy for your loved ones. It's just so haunting. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/skillerndoyle.jpg
"US Navy." . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/85990848/patrick-allen-randel 2nd Source: https://www.dps.texas.gov/memorial/narcotics-agent-patrick-allen-randel
I often have a lot of mixed emotions when going through last statements of executed people on death row. I’m not sympathetic necessarily, but I also don't view them as subhuman. Yes, these aren’t necessarily good people, but they are human. That comes with complexity. Charles Bass’ last statement, however, is one that just fills me with pure sadness. Maybe he did deserve his punishment, maybe he didn’t. But “I deserve this. Tell everyone I said goodbye.” Is just so… sad. I can respect that he owned his crime. He put the blame solely on himself. Maybe he should’ve elaborated on his remorse, but what more is there to say? He’s about to pay for his crimes with his life. I just hope everyone involved - both the victim and perpetrator’s surviving family have found some sort of peace. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/basscharles.jpg
The bio on find a grave talked more about his murder than his life. There is a headstone next to him from the 100 Club, but the picture was extremely hard to see. I could only make out this: "Honor [???} on police officer." . Image Source: https://the100club.org/heros/charles-baker/ Find A Grave: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/59928617/charles-henry-baker
In my previous posts, I've had people ask if I've ever seen a statement where the person just says they're ready to go. The answer to that is yes. I’ve seen many variations of this statement. A death row case can take over ten years to fully conclude. There comes a point where that time takes a heavy toll on you, and you’re just ready to be done. It's especially burdensome for someone who has a scheduled execution date, and is then granted a stay of execution on more than one occasion. Death row has an exhausting effect on everyone from all sides of the situation. Sometimes there’s peace at the end, or at the very least, acceptance. Sometimes you’re just ready to go, like Kenneth Brock. . Official Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/no_info_available.html Murderpedia was Much More Helpful: https://murderpedia.org/male.B/b1/brock-kenneth.htm
James Paster was involved in more than one hit for hire, by his own admission. In this murder, he was hired by Trudy Howard to kill her ex-husband, Robert Howard. James' last statement is a little bit ambiguous. When he mentions Mrs. Howard, he could be referring to Robert Howard's mother, or the wife that had him killed. It makes more sense for James to be referring to Robert's mother, but he could also be spiteful because Trudy was only sentenced to life imprisonment. I'm going to assume that he was referring to Robert Howard's mother. James only mentioned the surviving family of the victim - no mention of himself. This is the only last words I’ve seen thus far that summarized their offense to the living victims in a single sentence. This might seem cheesy, but some of the shortests statements carry the heaviest weight, provided that we are interpereting this statement correctly. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/pasterjames.jpg
Edit: A wonderful Imgurian helped me find a picture of him: https://imgur.com/a/Bphwuaq . Jerry Bird has pretty much been lost in time. He’s farther up the list of the 500+ executed inmates on death row (meaning you have to scroll to get to it) Murderpedia couldn’t produce a picture of him or his victim, and the only official acknowledgment of his existence on the TDCJ website is his last statement. Considering his invisibility, I’m going to guess that almost all memory of him has been erased, or at least a good amount of it. I wonder who last thought of him before I clicked on the links to his inmate information and last statement. HOWEVER, while the memory of Jerry Bird has faded into obscurity for us, he's forever burned into the minds of everyone who was affected by his crime. His mother, especially. . Official Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/no_info_available.html Murderpedia was Much More Helpful: https://murderpedia.org/male.B/b1/bird-jerry-joe.htm
Carl Kelly's last statement is the most original one I've ever encountered. I don't know if there would ever be another last statement like it. His words were blunt, yet poetic. It expressed a solid sense of self. They can be interpereted in two ways: indignance of his crime/conviction, or acceptance. I originally took it as indignance. Now I'm not so sure. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/kellycarl.jpg
Clifford Phillips is one of those losses that I don't feel too much, because of his disturbing outlook on the crime. His last statement is the epitome of insincerity and self-centeredness. In fact, I’d go so far as to say these are the words of a sociopath - a term that I do not use lightly. “Mishap” and “unfortunate incident” is an extremely arrogant and deflective way to describe “I strangled a 58 year old lady to death while robbing her.” The part about converting his wife is also disturbing. He speaks of her as if she is property, rather than a person. “My wife is very devoted.” To God, or you? This man talks as if he and God can be used interchangeably. I can’t read minds or see into the past, but I am extremely doubtful that he truly felt remorse. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/phillipsclifford.jpg
Iris Futor (or Futoransky) Siff, executive of the Alley Theatre in Houston, daughter of Israel G. and Sophia (Spitz) Futor, was born at Cushing, Oklahoma, on December 11, 1923. She attended Tyler Junior College in Texas, the Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México,(1942), and the University of Texas, from which she received a bachelor's degree in theater in 1944. After graduation she was employed as a radio field representative to South America by the Office of Inter-American Affairs. She married Alvin Siff on September 1, 1946; they had twin sons. Mrs. Siff joined the Alley Theatre as an actress in 1948, one year after its inception and just before its move from its original home on South Main to a factory it converted on Berry Street. She played the role of Mary in John Loves Mary, the last play on South Main, and she costumed the first show, The Children's Hour, in the theater's new home. In 1963 she was invited to testify before the United States Senate labor subcommittee on the arts, which was then working on legislation to establish the National Endowment for the Arts. After a period as a fashion coordinator and director of special services at the Sakowitz department store (1948-50, 1951-53), she returned to the Alley in 1964 to become the assistant of the theater's founder and director, Nina Vance. Iris Siff became managing director in 1968 and later conferred with architect Ulrich Franzen in developing a $5.5 million downtown theater complex. Her many accomplishments include the 1971 formation of the Alley's volunteer wing, the Alley Guild; the founding of the Alley Academy; and the introduction of the Studio School at Theatre, Incorporated, the Merry-Go-Round School at Casa Mañana in Fort Worth, and the Alley Merry-Go-Round. In 1980 she assumed both management and artistic operations upon Vance's death. Two years after having accepted control of the Alley, she [passed away]. . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/35648932/iris-siff
The reason that Harold Barnard's last few sentences were garbled was because the drugs were taking effect. The true last few words that he wanted the world to know while he still existed are lost in time because he was too far into the process of dying to be understood by the living. For me, that’s bone chilling. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/barnardharold.jpg
In previous posts, I also had people ask me if there are inmates who decline to make a last statement. The answer is yes to this as well. In fact, I would say it's the third most common type of last statement I've encountered. I chose George Lott's declining of a last statement for a specific reason. I was born and raised in Texas until I was 26, and moved to Massachussetts to flee the political climate of my home state. George Lott was executed on the day that my twin brother and I were born. Yes, that's probably coincidence that I'm reading way too much into. But it was weird to feel a calling to analyzing Texas death row statistics when a man was literally put to death on the day that I was brought into life. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/lottgeorge.jpg
Clyde Christopher Marshall Headstone Inscription: "A gentleman and a scholar." Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/65335865/clyde-christopher-marshall . I made a flower for John Edwards because I couldn't find a picture of him. I looked everwhere. I was honestly really surprised by that, given how infamous this incident originally was - not to mention this was the murder of an attorney.
Note: Website forgot to include that this was a written statement. . Joe F. Gonzales' case is extremely unique not just because of what he actually said, but because of the circumstances surrounding his execution. Joe Gonzales pled guilty to his crime, and clearly didn't try to bargain for life without parole in exchange for a guilty plea. When he was sentenced to death, he waived all of his appeals in favor of a speedy execution. He was executed 252 days later. It is to date the shortest amount of time that a condenmned inmate has spent on death row. That alone makes this a unique situation. Secondly, what he actually said in his written statement was also striking. "I will not ask for forgiveness, only acceptance." There is something striking about that, because it is true that forgiveness/acceptance are two separate things. You can accept that something happened without forgiving the person who did it to you. Wishing for acceptance is a profound expression of remorse. It doesn't ask the victim to give you anything. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/gonzalesjoe.jpg More About Him: https://www.upi.com/Archives/1996/09/18/Texas-executes-convicted-killer/6291843019200/
I've seen some snarky last statements, and Charlie Livingston's last words are high on that list. He was so straightforward with his impending death. He's also not wrong. He was there to be executed. I suppose he just wanted to get it over with. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/livingstoncharlie.jpg
Something that I see quite frequently in last statements is the condemned expressing love for everyone - not just their family, but to the victim’s family as well. Some even express love for the prison staff. I chose to feature this one because of its circumstances. Karla Tucker was the first woman to be executed in the State of Texas after capital punishment was reinstated. She never denied her crime, and that crime was HORRENDOUS. She was initially indignant, and then like many other people who are incarcerated, she converted to Christianity. Her new religion changed her outlook on herself, the crime, and the victims of her crime. However, all the reformation in the world cannot bring someone back to life. It’s also only a piece of redemption. Redemption also requires an acknowledgment of wrongdoings and paying your dues for them. Her execution wasn’t about punishing her present, it was about punishing her past. Whether death is an appropriate punishment for past behaviors is again, a question for another time. I’m not a religious person, and I don’t think I ever will be. I have issues with religion and rehabilitation, and that 's also a conversation for another time. That being said, sometimes it truly does help people. I don't believe that Karla is faking her remorse and reformation. I also greatly admire her reformation and graceful exit into the next life. I hope all the parties that were affected by her crime have found healing and peace. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/tuckerkarla.jpg
Jerry Lynn Dean: Bio: "YN3 U.S. Coast Guard" Nothing else is said about him, and his headstone only has his name and birth/death dates. Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/37452867/jerry_lynn-dean . Deborah "Debbie" Thornton Bio: Murder victim who was simply at the wrong place at the wrong time. Deborah had moved to the Houston area with her brother Ron Carlson to work in Bill List's trailer manufacturing company. Deborah was murdered along with Jerry Lynn Dean. Adoptive/step father. Homer D. Carlson Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/6843219/deborah_ruth-thornton
“It isn’t because I’m about to die” is another powerful statement to me. Joseph Cannon was at the end of the line. Nothing he could’ve said would alter his fate, and in that regard, he didn’t really owe anyone a last statement. It wasn’t going to change anything for him, but a dead man’s last words still have meaning for those who hear them. He didn’t just express genuine remorse, he did so with the acknowledgment that he is paying for his crimes with his own life. He wasn’t sorry for himself, he was sorry for the people he hurt. There are many people that are more sad about being caught doing something wrong. However, there are also people who have committed terrible transgressions that turn what would be pity for themselves into compassion for their victims. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/cannonjoseph.jpg
This one requires some context: While Kenneth McDuff was only officially put to death for the murder of one woman, he was actually a serial killer that the public coined "The Broomstick Killer." He's had at least eight known victims, but the authorities suspect there's more. If you're wondering why he was only put to death for Melissa Northrup's murder, it's because Melissa Northrtup was both his most recent victim, and the murder that the State of Texas could pursue a death penalty case against him. Many of his other victims were out of state. For those that aren't familiar with the American legal system: it works a little like Skyrim. You know how you can have a bounty in Whiterun but not in Riften? It's the same thing here. Unless the victim was murdered in The State of Texas, Texas can't pursue criminal charges - they have to be sent to the state where they committed the murder to be prosecuted for it. It's also sometimes the case that a person who is believed to be responsible for, let's say, six murders, is only tried for one of them. This might be because of a few reasons. Two big reasons are: the other charges were dropped as a result of a plea deal, or the prosecution doesn't think they have a strong enough case to bring the other five murders to trial. . . Kenneth McDuff's last words are an interesting sentiment that I've read before in serial killers. When I was working towards becoming a forensic neuropsychologist, I'd read about quite a few serial killers expressing being tired of killing, but also "don't know how to stop." In fact, some killers even get sloppier, as they essentially "burn out" on killing people. Tommy Linn Sells is an example of this. Coincidentally, he was also a serial killer that was tried and executed in Texas. When I originally wrote a caption for this, it unintentionally became a dissertation on antisocial personality disorder (sociopathy/psychopathy). I'm gonna try to shorten it to this: Kenneth McDuff unnerves me because every interview and picture I've come across with him, he only has one facial expression. Even Ted Bundy and Samuel Little were more expressive than him. Instead, he's constantly in an eerily still expression. It shows no emotion - not even the smirk that you see on many criminal psychopaths. This man just looks dead, all the time. I'm not surprised at all that he wants to be released from life. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/mcduffkenneth.jpg More About Him: https://murderpedia.org/male.M/m1/mcduff-kenneth.htm
This picture montage are his known victims, names ordered from left to right. Melissa Northrup's murder is the only one he was executed for. I'm only going to include Melissa's bio for length's sake, and I excluded the details about her murder just to make it more about Melissa's memory and not her death. Unfortunately this was the only thing that I could find: " Melissa was the dutiful mother of two small children and was pregnant for her third." Headstone Inscription: "Beloved mother." . Find A Grave: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/11637307/melissa-ann-northrup Victims Image Source: https://www.facebook.com/serialkillapp/photos/morning-guysa-really-good-friend-of-mine-and-a-loyal-page-follower-nell-has-a-bi/1082641148418046/
I was struck hard by Charles Tuttle's last words, because they were followed up with action. There are declarations of remorse, and there are actions that declare remorse. Charles Tuttle did both by dropping his last appeal, and explaining why in his last statement. Dropping his last appeal ensured that he would be executed. It was no longer a matter of “if” but “when," and that "when" would come fairly quickly after. It was the only thing he could have done for the surviving family. He was sentenced to death, and he couldn't bring Cathy Harris back. Cutting to the chase allowed them more time to heal and move on. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/tuttlecharles.jpg
I shared this last statement not just because it was so compassionate, but also as an offset to Thomas Barefoot’s last words. “Don’t let it come between you and God” are the words of someone who truly feels remorse for their actions and wants the best for the surviving family, especially spiritually. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/cranealvin.jpg
Jack Clark's last statement is another unique one because he invited the surviving family of the woman he brutally murdered to his funeral. Some might take this as “a textbook narcissist being smug for the last time,” especially given the heinousness of his crime, but I don’t see it that way. He’s had twelve years to sit on this crime in a place where there’s not much else to do but reflect on his life and past actions. I think he was extending a strange, but genuine olive branch. . Jack Clark: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/clarkjack.jpg
No bio, but her headstone is in the shape of a heart. It says: "In loving memory of Melissa Ann Garcia. [birth/death dates]. From mom, dad, sisters & brothers." . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/10030936/melisa-ann-garcia
"If I was y'all, I would have killed me." Words spoken from the man who is about to be executed for his unspeakable crime. In fact, the crime was so unspeakable that the offender himself didn't know what to say except a repeated "I'm sorry," and commenting on how his victims didn't deserve it. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/dowthittdennis.jpg
Both Girls' Obituary: "Tiffany/Grace was a victim of homicide. A young life taken far too soon." Grace's Headstone: "Sweet sixteen forever." Tiffany's Headstone: "Baby Mouse" . Grace Purnhagen: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/7860798/grace-marie-purnhagen Tiffany Purnhagen: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/7860764/tiffany-elena-purnhagen
He's right. The death penalty brings closure to many people, but it’s also a fact that in a death penalty case, EVERYONE dies. It’s death that’s being reimbursed with more death. When everything ends in a loss of life, it’s a damn tragedy. Whether the offender deserved to pay for his crimes with his life is again, a question I'm not going to get into on this post. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/brousardwindell.jpg
This one also deeply saddened me. Monty Delk was a paranoid schizophrenic. It's tragic when one is so detached from reality that the last thing they are able to say before they cease to exist is incomprehensible chatter. . . . This is another case rife with controversy. Monty Delk was originally considered to be incompetent enough for the death penalty due to paranoid schizophrenia. This was generally accepted until 1994, when Delk was allegedly caught bragging to other inmates about faking his condition. I'm not sure about that. Paranoid schizophrenia isn't just hallucinations, paranoia, and delusions. It's also a thought disorder. There are heavy neurological deficits (problems) at play. This isn't ALWAYS the case, but severe paranoid schizophrenia often comes with a demeanor that's unmistakeable. They're unkempt, and their facial expression is either flat or highly agitated. Yes, it's possible to fake severe paranoid schizophrenia, but it's exceedingly difficult. I read another case file on Monty Delk, which included another mugshot (this was after I made this image, which is why it's not on here). I would be 100% SHOCKED if he didn't actually have paranoid schizophrenia. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/delkmonty.jpg Second Case File: https://tcadp.org/wp-content/uploads/2010/06/monty-delk-case-study2.pdf
Obituary: GENE OLAN "BUBBA" ALLEN, II The son of Gene J. Allen & Sandra Sue (Carpenter) Allen Married to Sheila Carpenter Tombstone Inscription: "What hopes have perished with you my son, brother, husband, and father." . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/41559263/gene-olan-allen
Beginning Note: I know the internet can bring out the middle schooler in us, and we laugh at sexual innuendos. "Jerry Nutt" is a name that I could see the internet running off with. Now is not the time, and not with this man. Jerry Nutt is not the punchline of a sex joke, he's a man who lost his entire family and the house that was full of memories of them. . . You can't un-kill someone. You can't un-burn a house down. No amount of remorse, retrospection, reform, or redemption can undo what's already done. Unlike the future, the past is written in stone. Jermaine violently took a man's wife and son away from him, but he didn't ask for his forgiveness. He wished Mr. Jerry Nutt peace. To me, his last statement was all about ensuring the wellbeing of the man who's life he utterly destroyed, and to not hold onto hate that would only make his life more miserable. There hundreds of statements that express what I believe to be genuine remorse. Jermaine Herron's is one of the most gentle, kind, and reassuring final statements I've ever read. That being said, this doesn't mean that Jerry Nutt has an obligation to forgive him. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/herronjermaine.html
This is similar to what I said about Jermaine Herron's last statement, but not quite. I get the impression that Jonathan Moore murdered Fabian Dominguez on impulse rather than deliberation. One could argue that shooting Officer Dominguez three more times shows deliberation, but from what I have personally learned about human behavior, impulsivity isn't just one split-second decision. It can be five split-second decisions in a row. Jonathan Moore's last statement was actually painful to me because I don't think he would've been on that gurney if he was sober and hadn't panicked. Impulsivity damages lives far more than deliberation, because they're made in a blurry "heat of the moment". For the offender, the entirety of a devastating situation isn't fully realized until everything is over and the dust settles. Then comes the weight of what they've done. Loss of life by your hands is a heavy weight to carry, especially by impulse. An impulsive murder is a murder that you know might have been avoided had you kept your composure. I can imagine that sting of regret was more like a boulder crushing Jonathan Moore's conscience. You can feel it in his apology to Officer Dominguez's widow, Jennifer Dominguez. "I saw his face and his smile and I knew he was a good man." Now that good man is gone, and Jonathan Moore is about to join him. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/moorejohnathan.jpg
Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/114339610/fabian-dale-dominguez
Some inmates go down swinging, and Joseph Nichols is one of the more prevalent examples of this. I’m not sure if I would want my legacy to be finalized as “profanity towards staff,” but being strapped to the gurney is an intense and terrifying experience. Fight/flight is disabled because you’re frozen in place by force. The only way to fight at this point is to pick a verbal fight with the other party, which was pretty much what Joseph Nichols did. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/nicholsjoseph.jpg
James Clark was clearly caught off guard with the knowledge that there were people at his execution. . . . There is controvery about James Clark's execution - more than the usual controversy around capital punishment. Texas has a ruling that people with severe intellectual disabilities can't be executed because they lack full comprehension of their actions. James Clark's crime was horrendous, but he was also severely intellectually disabled. Two neuropsychologists testified that he had an IQ of 68. The average IQ is 90 - 100. His updated mugshot is also concerning. Eyes don't naturally rest like that. That being said, he was neurologically sound enough to participate in an elaborate murder and sexual assault. However, there was an accomplice that was guiding him along. He could've easily been set up for having a bigger role in the crime than he actually played. I couldn't quote James without adding this extra detail, but I'm not in any position to make an opinion about his culpability in the crime. . . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/clarkjames.html Prosecutor's Website: http://www.clarkprosecutor.org/html/death/US/clark1070.htm *Note, I was mistaken on the website. It's *A* prosecutor's website that follows death penalty cases - not THE prosecutor that put him on trial.
Obituary: Cari's father is a retired lieutenant colonel in the Air Force, so she lived in several places in her life: Omaha, Nebraska; Bitburg, Germany; Fairfax, Virginia; Montgomery, Alabama; Maryland's Andrews Air Base; and Boston. She had lived in Denton for the last four years and had attended Strickland Junior High. She was a member of the National Honor Society and was ranked among the top 2 percent of her junior class. She was elected president of her school's chapter of Amnesty International for next year, was selected for Who's Who in American High Schools and won first place in a short-story contest at Cooke County Junior College. She was an avid environmentalist and a classical pianist. She also volunteered at the Denton Humane Society and provided a foster home for homeless animals. She was survived by her parents, Earl and Risa Crews of Denton; brother, Alexander Earl Crews of Denton; grandparents, Harry Dobbs and LaVerne Kilgore, both of Fort Worth, and Marie Crews of Sherman; and great-grandmother, Annie Keller of Goldthwaite. Cari and her classmate and friend, Jesus Garza, were the victims of a homicide. Cari's marker includes the quotation, "Her life, a beautiful gift to us, brief but remarkable." . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/18893208/shari-catherine-crews
I was the one that typed the summary in the image. I included Michael Perry because of his noteriety. Werner Herzog did a documentary about Michael Perry called “Into The Abyss.” He titled it as such because he felt that looking into Perry’s eyes was like looking into an abyss. He subsequently said that Perry was the most dangerous man he'd ever encountered. I don't blame him for saying that. Michael Perry's dangerousness is especially apparent when you learn that he murdered three people to steal a red camaro while sober. The officers that were interviewed in “Into the Abyss” said that they didn’t think alcohol or drugs were involved. This calculated plan to kill three people to obtain a clicker to steal a car is a crime was done in a sound state of mind. Not only was it senseless, it lacked any conscience. About his last statement specifically: I don't think Michael realized that his forgiveness held no weight when compared to the sheer viciousness of his crime. Nobody was seeking his approval or forgiveness, mentioning it was meaningless. . Official Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/perrymichael.html Into the Abyss: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QmyN3QJky7I
I screenshot these from "Into the Abyss." I couldn't find a decent obituary for any one of them, unfortunately.
Currently, there are 588 executed inmates since capital punishment was reinstated in the State of Texas. I've read all of them. I also went through last statements of famous utterly sadistic serial killers, and last statements of executed inmates from other states. Basically, I've read close to 1,000 last statements of executed people, maybe even more. I have never, EVER, read "If I hadn't raped you..." before. Even worse, he followed it up with "If I hadn't raped you, then you wouldn't have lived." Even Ted Bundy, who committed far more heinous crimes, had more tact than Rickey Lewis. "I thank the Lord for the man I am today" is especially disturbing because if he's an improved man since he was first arrested, I shudder to think of what monstrosity he was before. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/lewisrickey.html
I couldn't find an obituary. Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/50168470/george-ray-newman
It was a clever play on words. There’s also truth to it - many people on death row came from a poor socioeconomic background. That being said, this last statement struck me because of the man it belonged to. First off, John King didn’t come up with that statement. The first person to say it was Bryan Stevenson in his book “Just Mercy,” published in 2014. Bryan Stevenson is black attorney who has been working to abolish capital punishment. It’s a bit ironic that an openly white supremecist that sadistically lynched a black man is quoting another black man in his final written statement. I also don’t feel like his lack of capital had anything to do with his punishment - it was the sheer evilness of his crime. . Inmate Information: https://www.tdcj.texas.gov/death_row/dr_info/kingjohn.html More About Him: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/apr/24/texas-execution-john-william-king-james-byrd
James Byrd Jr. was the third of nine children born to Stella Mae Sharp and James Byrd Sr. The family was heavily involved in their local church community. He was a graduate of the last segregated class of Jasper Rowe High School in 1967. After graduation, he worked as a vacuum salesman, married, and had three children. [I excluded the part that details the murder] Byrd was buried near an old iron fence on the Black side of Jasper City Cemetery. That fence, which separated the Black and white graves for more than 150 years, was torn down in 1999. Headstone Inscription: "My beloved son." . Source: https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/7105192/james-byrd
item.dc:creator: OlivetheNerd

item.title: Smol dump of legally stolen memes and things pt 6
item.description:
They know.
Hello.
But they never want to give it to me.
I have many glass jars. The tinier, the better.
Me trying to get out of work.
LFG
It's better to know these things about yourself.
Nope.
It's for the children.
Kermit a bad MF.
Festival season us upon us.
Be yourself.
Who are you listening to right now? Intimate with Imgur.
Monday Mood
My boss had a bird shit on him the other day. Wonder where that bird is now?
I may have been intoxicated.
Shit.
Cat Tax
item.dc:creator: WorthlessRaisin

item.title: Time for yet another unhinged XL Monday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
that's too much for me but the pizza looks very good
corgis dgaf
a lot to unpack here
heck yes
wait what
i knew it wasn't pineapple juice
i don't see a reblog button
it's a door. doors = exits. it's an exit.
we've all been there
no. yes.
ocean is absolutely a fuck
hey it's ed and lorraine warren
purple is the only choice right?
mmm hmm
oh cool a personal attack on me
random ass movie
that's my set.. have a great night gang!!!

item.title: "Remember that anything worth doing is worth doing half-assed." K.C. Davis
item.description: 'In fact, I do not think laziness exists. You know what does exist? Executive dysfunction, procrastination, feeling overwhelmed, perfectionism, trauma, amotivation, chronic pain, energy fatigue, depression, lack of skills, lack of support, and differing priorities." "No one ever shamed themselves into better mental health." K.C. Davis, How to Keep House While Drowning

item.title: A little bit late there, Mouse.
item.description: https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/19/business/disney-arbitration-wrongful-death-lawsuit-intl-hnk/index.html The correct time to make this decision would've been at the moment they were all in the courtroom for the first time. This is what happens when you let a shark culture in the office dominate for too long - it becomes normalized and it ends up hurting the wrong people. Who wants to bet the PR department bludgeoned the living daylights out of Legal?

item.dc:creator: IMSALTYRICK

item.title: Jasmine’s plans fell through
item.description:
I promised y’all an update on Jasmine today and here it is: the family who adopted her brother Abu showed an interest in adopting her, too. He needed a buddy and they were excited about giving him one. But they weren’t happy about waiting another week for her to be ready, so the shelter told me that they adopted another kitten this morning. I’m pretty disappointed, because that would have been a perfect situation for everyone. But it is what it is.
Now her future is up in the air. I know Imgur is united in their desire for her to be a foster fail for me. But I’ve got to give her a chance to find another family who will love and cherish her. That’s literally my job as a kitten fosterer. The shelter said they can’t ensure that someone who adopts her from there will keep in touch with me and provide updates, so I’m going to be reaching out to Facebook, Instagram, Imgur, etc to see if I can find an adopter who will be willing to provide updates and allow me to watch her grow up. I’m invested in her and want what’s best for her. Any Imgurian close to Huntsville, Alabama who wants this baby girl?? Edit: I have been chatting for the past half hour with an Imgurian who lives in town who is VERY interested in adopting Jasmine. Ahhhhh!!! I am so excited. I’ll update tomorrow!
item.dc:creator: KittensForDays

item.title: Voice Actress Atsuko Tanaka has passed away at age 61
item.description:
Some of her most famous roles include Motoko Kusanagi in "Ghost in the Shell" (the original film and Stand Alone Complex), Lisa Lisa in "JoJo’s Bizarre Adventures", Konan in "Naruto", Bayonetta in "Bayonetta", Flamme in "Frieren: Beyond Journey’s End" ... and many more She also was the main dub actress for American shows/films that aired in Japan. She dubbed especially for Nicole Kidman - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atsuko_Tanaka_%28voice_actress%29#Dubbing_roles
On August 20, 2024, my mother, the voice actress Atsuko Tanaka, peacefully passed away. To all the fans who loved Atsuko Tanaka, To everyone in the industry who worked with her during her lifetime, I would like to express my deepest gratitude on behalf of her. It is with great regret that I must inform you of this parent-child relationship in such a manner. As per her wishes, I will not disclose the specific illness, but she bravely fought it for over a year with grace, humor, and strength. I believe it was a life that was truly characteristic of Atsuko Tanaka. I feel truly blessed to have had such a proud mother. Lastly, Please do not forget that Atsuko Tanaka poured her heart and soul into the characters she loved and brought to life. And Atsuko Tanaka, the voice actress, herself. With love and respect for the woman and voice actress that was my mother, I sign this message with love. August 20, 2024 Hikaru Tanaka
item.dc:creator: Pasquinel

item.title: Feeling bummed
item.description: I just feel alone. I always take the time to wish my friends and family happy birthday and make them feel loved and thought of but I never feel like it’s never returned. Today not one person has even just sent a text saying happy birthday. Sometimes I just feel like I put in so much effort to friendships and family who I’m not even an afterthought to them. I don’t know. Sometimes I think Imgur is the only family I have
item.dc:creator: kosmokramertheassman

item.title: The time has come to clear the phone and share some smiles - cat edition.
item.description: EDIT: Whoa! Woke up to Most Viral and Accolades - what a warm and fuzzy way to start the day. Happy to share the smiles. And thanks to those that shared insight about #5. Always good to learn about our feline friends.

item.title: 5-Big FU
item.description:
FOLFERI - iykyk
After that it goes into a bit of a decline.
Chemo seems physically and emotionally cumulative - smells are particular triggering - but I’m now able to binge all the things. Found 24 hour MST3K and ST:TOS streams. Not sure which legal weed / gummies to take yet, but I’ve got options. And this is just void shouting, mind you. As a devout atheist, I have achieved immortality preserved in formalin.
item.dc:creator: sleestacks
